## [15:08 - 18:08]

without asking his customers what they wanted because they would have preferred faster horses. So in that convince us, you have to convince them that this is a better solution or this is a solution to a problem and actually then start to describe this brand new product as well. And some of the key things to be looking out for in communicating and engaging in your ad is what are the goals of your user? What are they trying to get that you can offer? What are their struggles? If it's a mom and you've got a product that helps mom to stay organized, then the struggle is around organization. So how can you focus on organization? Or maybe it's a false belief, maybe there's a product that you're selling or a service that you're selling, which is gonna help solve a problem for them, but they have a different mindset around it. So how can you persuade them and bridge the gap between what they believe to be the case, which is not the case and you're gonna convince them otherwise? Or perhaps what's the opportunity cost? What's the cost of inaction? If they don't take your product or your service or your offer, what's gonna happen? If you don't buy that lorry ticket, the opportunity cost is you're not gonna win lorry. So it's kind of thinking about how you can translate that into your products and service and get that point across that if you don't take this action today, or if you don't buy this product, or if you don't click here, you're not gonna get the benefits that you want out of things that they're gonna lie for, the products, et cetera. And also kind of things like impulsiveness as well. If you've got a low ticket value product, then impulsiveness is a big part of convincing people to buy as well. So a lot of people who are selling low ticket e-commerce products, they really push for on impulsiveness. So $20, $30 products, it doesn't need too much thinking to part with that much money. And so they play a lot on impulsiveness. So using a lot of social proof, some really cool videos, and trying to get that person to engage and click through as well. And one of the most important things I look at when I'm looking at the customer, the product and the proposition is Maslow's hierarchy. So if you haven't heard of this before, Maslow was a behavioral scientist, I guess, and he was kind of studying as a psychologist, sorry. He was studying the human behavior and the different qualities that people have and they look for as well. And he's created this kind of layered pyramid, which goes through the most basic needs. So right at the bottom of the pyramid, up to things which are aspirational. And a lot of it goes also from the physical to the non-physical as well. And actually, if you relate your product or service

## [18:08 - 21:08]

to one part of this pyramid, it also relates to how you need to communicate the product or service as well. So if you're selling something related to food or water or sleep, then people know naturally what that is. You don't need to explain that this is food. This is how you consume food. This is the benefit of food. We kind of all know that, or the benefit of sleep. People generally know that you have to sleep. You've got no choice, but it also helps you to regenerate, recharge, et cetera. So there may be a few things you can skip. Now as you go up that hierarchy, things like self-esteem or confidence or morality or some kind of sense of achievement, some maybe friendship, family, all that kind of stuff is a bit more intangible now. So when you're describing the product or service, you have to try and make it a bit more tangible and relate it to the person you're speaking to. So therefore the approach changes. And generally what I find is as you go up the hierarchy here, you end up elongating the content, the funnel, the communication, because it takes that much longer or it's that much harder to actually get your point across as well. So if you're selling, let's say something which is gonna help people become a lot more self-confident and self-confident within themselves, then you need to take them through a completely different journey to selling food parcels or food packages or whatever it be. And so the kind of copy has to be a bit more inspirational. It has to connect a lot better. You're probably gonna end up using a long copy. You're probably gonna end up maybe using email capture and taking them through an email journey, et cetera. So I like to reflect products on the kind of hierarchy of needs as well. So it's just something to think about when you're coming up with your ads and your engagement as well. And the kind of last few parts of this is then breaking out the appeal and the connect. So when you're trying to appeal to them, appeal to their emotional state, especially if it's a higher ticket offer or if it's a less tangible product or service, speak to the emotional state, sorry, appeal to the emotional state, but then speak to their functional state. So appealing to their emotional state might be, let's say a dating service and you're lonely or you wanna meet that perfect partner and you want to have a wonderful married life. That's the kind of emotional state of it and that's kind of how you can appeal to the emotional state. The emotional state is embedded deeper in the mind, in the brain. And so when you can cut through to that deeper part of the brain and kind of a deeper response, you're more likely to get someone hooked and engaged and then speak to the functional state. So here's a dating app or give us your email address. We'll send you some dating tips. What I find is a lot of people go straight to the functional. They miss the emotional out and then wonder why their ad doesn't work.

slash policies slash ads and have a look through.
